cards.front: Explain the goal of DFS in a binary tree.
cards.back: DFS (Depth-First Search) explores a binary tree by going as deep as possible along a branch before backtracking. The goal is to fully process each subtree before moving to sibling nodes. It is commonly used for problems that require aggregating results from children, exploring paths, or computing values such as height, path sums, or subtree properties.

cards.front: Explain the goal of BFS in a binary tree.
cards.back: BFS (Breadth-First Search) explores the tree level by level starting from the root. The goal is to process nodes in order of their distance from the root. It is commonly used for level-order traversal, shortest path in unweighted trees, and problems involving levels (e.g., average of each level, right-side view).

cards.front: Explain the behavior of DFS in a binary tree.
cards.back: DFS starts at the root and explores one subtree completely before visiting the other. The traversal follows a branch down to a leaf, then backtracks to explore other branches. DFS is typically implemented using recursion or an explicit stack.

cards.front: Explain the behavior of BFS in a binary tree.
cards.back: BFS starts at the root and visits nodes level by level. It processes all nodes at the current depth before moving to the next level. BFS is implemented using a queue where children are added as their parents are processed.

cards.front: Explain the properties of BFS in a binary tree.
cards.back:
• Traverses nodes level by level.
• Uses a queue data structure.
• Processes nodes in order of distance from the root.
• Naturally groups nodes by levels.
• Useful for shortest-path problems in unweighted trees and level-based problems.
• Time complexity: O(n).
• Space complexity: O(w), where w is the maximum width of the tree.

cards.front: Explain pre-order traversal using DFS in a binary tree. What are its properties?
cards.back:
Pre-order traversal processes nodes in the order: Root → Left → Right. The current node is visited before its children.

Properties:
• The root is processed before its subtrees.
• Useful for copying or serializing a tree.
• Can be used to reconstruct a tree when combined with in-order traversal.

cards.front: Write pseudocode for pre-order traversal using DFS in a binary tree.
Explain in-order traversal using DFS in a binary tree. What are its properties?
cards.back:
In-order traversal processes nodes in the order: Left → Root → Right.

Properties:
• The left subtree is processed before the node.
• The right subtree is processed after the node.
• In a Binary Search Tree (BST), in-order traversal visits nodes in sorted order.

cards.front: Write pseudocode for in-order traversal using DFS in a binary tree.
Explain post-order traversal using DFS in a binary tree. What are its properties?
cards.back:
Post-order traversal processes nodes in the order: Left → Right → Root.

Properties:
• Both subtrees are processed before the node itself.
• Useful when results depend on values computed in children.
• Commonly used for deleting trees, computing heights, or dynamic programming on trees.

cards.front: Write pseudocode for post-order traversal using DFS in a binary tree.
Explain the behavior of a recursive bottom-up pattern in a binary tree. What are its properties?
cards.back:
The bottom-up pattern processes children first and then computes the result for the current node using results returned from its children.

Properties:
• Uses post-order traversal.
• Each recursive call returns information about its subtree.
• The parent combines results from the left and right children.
• Commonly used for problems like computing tree height, diameter, balanced tree checks, or subtree sums.

cards.front: Write pseudocode for the recursive bottom-up pattern in a binary tree.
cards.back:
function dfs(node):
    if node == null:
        return base_value

    left = dfs(node.left)
    right = dfs(node.right)

    result = combine(left, right, node)

    return result

cards.front: Explain how to calculate the path sum in a binary tree.
cards.back: To check whether a root-to-leaf path equals a target sum, recursively track the accumulated sum while traversing the tree. At each node add its value to the running sum. When a leaf node is reached, check whether the accumulated sum equals the target. The recursion explores all root-to-leaf paths.

cards.front: Write pseudocode to calculate the path sum in a binary tree.
Explain how to calculate the maximum path sum (any node to any node) in a binary tree.
cards.back:
Use a DFS bottom-up approach. For each node compute the maximum gain from its left and right child (ignore negative contributions). The best path passing through the node is:

node.value + left_gain + right_gain

Track the global maximum. The recursive call returns the best single-branch gain to the parent:

node.value + max(left_gain, right_gain)

cards.front: Explain how to construct a binary tree from pre-order and in-order traversal.
cards.back: The first element in the preorder traversal is the root. Find this value in the inorder traversal. All values to the left of it belong to the left subtree, and all values to the right belong to the right subtree. The number of nodes in the left subtree tells us how to split the preorder list into left and right subtree segments. Recursively repeat this process to build the tree.

cards.front: What does the pre-order traversal tell you when reconstructing a binary tree?
cards.back: Pre-order traversal tells you the root of each subtree. Once the left and right subtree ranges are known, the first element in the preorder sublist is always the root of that subtree.

cards.front: What does the in-order traversal tell you when reconstructing a binary tree?
cards.back: In-order traversal tells you how the tree splits into left and right subtrees. The root value divides the inorder list into two parts: elements on the left belong to the left subtree, and elements on the right belong to the right subtree.
